content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 15, 2017

Daniel G. Sodergren
City Attorney
City of Pleasanton
P.O. Box 520
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-104

Dear Mr. Sodergren:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Pleasanton City Councilmember Ame Olson regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to a potential project to improve roads in the general vicinity of his residence that could lead to a significant increase in traffic at an intersection that the Councilmember must pass through to get to and from his residence?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the potential project because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real-property interest in his residence.

FACTS

You are the Pleasanton City Attorney and the authorized representative of City Councilmember Ame Olson. The City Council recently adopted its 2017-2018 City Council Work Plan Priorities. One of the Council's priorities is to initiate a feasibility study and traffic modeling

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-104
Page No. 2

project in partnership with the Alameda County Transportation Commission for possible
improvements to El Charro Road. The project would assess feasibility of construction, funding
options, and traffic impacts and mitigations for Council review and consideration.

The El Charro Road improvements would result in an arterial roadway from Stoneridge
Drive south to Busch Road. Busch Road would also be improved as a collector roadway from
Valley Avenue to El Charro Road. Stoneridge Drive is a major thoroughfare in the City with
extensive commercial development including the Stoneridge Mall. The intersection of Busch Road
and Ironwood Drive is within the section of Busch Road that the project would improve.

The Councilmember owns his residence on Chatham Court within the Village at Ironwood,
a gated community for adults age 55 or older. The Councilmember's residence is approximately
one-half mile from the potential project's planned improvements to El Charro Road and
approximately 925 feet from its planned improvements to Busch Road.

Ironwood Drive is the only road that provides access to and from the portion of the Village
at Ironwood where the Councilmember's residence is located. To the north, Ironwood Drive
terminates at a three-way intersection with Donahue Drive and Ironwood Court; to the south, it
terminates at Busch Road, within the area of Busch Road subject to the potential project's
improvements. According to the City's Traffic Engineer, the improvements to El Charro Road and
Busch Road could result in an approximate net increase of PM Peak Hour trips at the intersection of
Busch Road and Ironwood Drive from 90 to 545, more than a 500 percent increase.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. Of the
interests identified in Section 87103, the decisions at issue and the facts presented implicate his
real-property interest in his residence, assuming he has invested $2,000 or more in that property.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding
if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit,
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the official's interest
is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18701(b) provides that the decision's effect on
the official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and
more than hypothetical or theoretical.

The Councilmember's real-property interest is not explicitly involved in the decisions
relating to the potential project. Therefore, the effect of those decisions is reasonably foreseeable if
it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

File No. A-17-104
Page No. 3

Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest is material whenever the decision:

"Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision;

"Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; or

"Would cause a reasonably prudent person using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property."

(Regulation 18702.2(a)(6), (10), and (12), respectively.)

The potential improvements to El Charro Road and Busch Road could result in an approximate net increase of PM Peak Hour trips at the intersection of Busch Road and Ironwood Drive from 90 to 545, more than a 500 percent increase. And that intersection is the only road that provides access to and from the Councilmember's residence. Therefore, the Councilmember could receive a disproportionate detriment from the decisions at issue because those decisions could substantially affect the traffic at this intersection, which the Councilmember is reliant upon for access to and from his residence.

Furthermore, given that Ironwood Drive is the only road that provides access to and from the Councilmember's residence, the potential 500 plus percent increase of PM Peak Hour trips at the intersection of Busch Road and Ironwood Drive indicates the decisions at issue could change the character of the Councilmember's residence by substantially altering traffic levels of property surrounding it in a manner that could alter or influence its market value.

Accordingly, we conclude that the Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the potential project to improve El Charro Road and Busch Road because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real-property interest in his residence.

File No. A-17-104
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature: Matthew F. Christy]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFCjgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to a 
potential project to improve roads in the general vicinity of his residence that could lead to a 
significant increase in traffic at an intersection that the Councilmember must pass through to get to 
and from his residence?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the 
potential project because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on his real-property interest in his residence.

Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on a

drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an interest 
will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from 
improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly 
situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will 
otherwise receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision;

altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property

potential project to improve roads in the general vicinity of his residence that could lead to a

and from his residence?
